skill.name: vercel-cli-with-tokens
skill.description: Deploy and manage Vercel projects with the Vercel CLI using token-based authentication instead of interactive login.
skill.summary: Guide agents through token-based Vercel CLI setup, deployment, linking, environment variables, domains, and troubleshooting.
skill.author: vercel-labs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly detected extensive external command guidance, local file access, and credential handling. Review found no prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent, but token lookup commands can print secrets and the skill directs agents to run deployment, git, and Vercel environment-management commands with real side effects.

medium_findings.title: Token Discovery Can Expose Secrets
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to print VERCEL_TOKEN and grep .env files. This is legitimate setup guidance, but it can expose access tokens in terminal output, logs, or AI transcripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly read token-related environment variables and .env entries. The skill also warns against passing tokens as flags, so the risk is exposure during discovery rather than malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: External Deployment Commands Have Real Side Effects
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to install the Vercel CLI, deploy projects, link projects, push git commits, and manage Vercel environment variables. These commands are expected for the skill, but mistakes can alter production resources or publish unintended code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and operational, including deploy, git push, and environment variable changes. The surrounding text includes safeguards, such as preview defaults and asking before push, which lowers severity.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Benign Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded Vercel URL findings are example project URLs used to explain team slug extraction. They are not outbound fetches or credential destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines show documentation text and shell parsing of a user-provided PROJECT_URL. No evidence found of sending data to that URL.

low_findings.title: Local Project Metadata Reads Are Expected
low_findings.description: The skill checks .vercel project metadata and git remote configuration to identify project state. This is expected for deployment automation but reveals local project identifiers to the agent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file reads are limited to deployment metadata and git remote details. No evidence found of broad filesystem traversal or hidden data collection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands Handling Credentials
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines shell commands with token and .env access. This is necessary for token-based CLI use, but agents should avoid displaying secret values and should redact outputs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact examples read or export Vercel credentials. The pattern is semantically credential handling, not just a scanner artifact.

dangerous_patterns.title: Non-Interactive Cloud Resource Changes
dangerous_patterns.description: Several commands use -y or automated CLI operations for deploys, links, domains, and environment variables. These operations can change remote Vercel state without interactive confirmation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clear Vercel operations with side effects. The skill has some consent guardrails, so this is a dangerous pattern to manage rather than evidence of malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Interactive Vercel login can block automated agent workflows. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured token-based path for deploys, linking, environment variables, domains, and troubleshooting.
actual_capabilities: Finds Vercel token, org, and project identifiers from environment variables or local .env files.
Installs and checks the Vercel CLI before deployment work begins.
Guides preview and production deployments with token-based authentication.
Links local or cloned repositories to Vercel projects using team scope information.
Manages Vercel environment variables, deployment inspection, logs, and domains.
Provides troubleshooting steps for token, team, build, and CLI installation issues.
limitations: Requires a valid Vercel access token with permissions for the target team and project.
Can expose secrets if token lookup command output is not redacted.
Does not replace human approval for production deploys, git pushes, or domain changes.
Depends on the Vercel CLI behavior and project configuration available in the local environment.

use_cases.title: Preview Deployment From Local Code
use_cases.description: Deploy a local project to Vercel preview without using browser-based login.

use_cases.title: Team Project Setup
use_cases.description: Link a repository to the correct Vercel team and project using scoped CLI commands.

use_cases.title: Environment Variable Maintenance
use_cases.description: List, add, pull, or remove Vercel environment variables through a guided CLI workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy a Preview
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Vercel token workflow to deploy this project as a preview. Redact any token values you read or print.

prompt_templates.title: Link a Team Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up this repository for Vercel CLI deployment under the correct team scope. Ask before any command that changes remote state.

prompt_templates.title: Update Environment Variables
prompt_templates.prompt: Add or update the Vercel environment variables I provide for preview and production. Confirm the target project before applying changes.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot Failed Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Investigate this failed Vercel deployment using token-based CLI commands. Check authentication, team scope, logs, and project configuration.

output_examples.input: Deploy this app to Vercel preview using the token in my environment.
output_examples.output: The agent verifies that authentication and project scope are available, runs a preview deployment, and returns the deployment URL with token values redacted.

output_examples.input: Link this repository to the Vercel project for my team.
output_examples.output: The agent checks git remote information, selects the team scope, links the project, and reports the resulting local Vercel metadata without exposing secrets.

output_examples.input: Add DATABASE_URL to production on Vercel.
output_examples.output: The agent confirms the target project and environment, sends the value through the Vercel CLI, and summarizes the completed change.

best_practices: Redact token values and environment variable values from command output before sharing results.
Use preview deployments by default and require explicit approval before production changes.
Confirm team scope, project identity, and repository state before linking or deploying.
anti_patterns: Do not pass Vercel tokens as command-line flags or include them in shell history.
Do not push git commits, change domains, or deploy to production without explicit user approval.
Do not run broad environment dumps when a narrower token check is enough.

faq.question: Does this skill require interactive Vercel login?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for token-based authentication through VERCEL_TOKEN and related project variables.

faq.question: Can it deploy to production?
faq.answer: Yes, but the workflow says production deploys should only happen after an explicit user request.

faq.question: Will it read local .env files?
faq.answer: Yes. It may inspect .env files to find Vercel tokens and project identifiers, so outputs should be redacted.

faq.question: Can it manage Vercel environment variables?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes workflows for listing, adding, pulling, and removing Vercel environment variables.

faq.question: Does it support team-scoped projects?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains how to use team slugs, VERCEL_ORG_ID, and VERCEL_PROJECT_ID for scoped operations.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The main concern is secret exposure during token discovery. Agents should avoid printing or storing token values.
